summary: Six months after forking the deprecated godoc.org into godocs.io, the project has made significant improvements while preserving the original experience. The fork was motivated by pkg.go.dev's restrictive license policies that excluded many projects from documentation display. A small team of volunteers added Go modules support, Gemini protocol access, and a PostgreSQL search backend. They also removed 64% of the original codebase, eliminating Google infrastructure dependencies and making self-hosting practical.
key_points: pkg.go.dev's narrow license whitelist for displaying documentation motivated the fork
The team removed 37,000 lines of code (64% of the codebase) while adding new features
Google infrastructure dependencies were stripped out, making local and intranet deployment feasible
A small number of volunteers accomplished all improvements, demonstrating the power of community-driven maintenance
money_quotes: The new website isn't right for many projects — one of the most glaring issues is the narrow list of software licenses pkg.go.dev will display documentation for.
We also substantially cleaned up the codebase, removing over 37,000 lines of code — 64% of the lines from the original code base.
The third-party dependencies to Google infrastructure have been removed and it's much easier to run the software locally or on your intranet, too.
Thanks to the efforts of just a small number of volunteers.
We're happy to be supporting the Go community with this tool, and looking forward to making more (conservative!) improvements in the future.
key_insight: When a corporate steward deprecates community infrastructure and replaces it with something more restrictive, a small group of motivated volunteers can fork, simplify, and improve it to better serve the community.
